APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses end-to-end ASR via a joint CTC-attention model, which is squarely within the remit of top-tier NLP/ML conferences. It evaluates on standard speech benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compares to strong, relevant baselines (hybrid HMM/DNN, CTC variants, TDNN with LF-MMI), using community-expected methods and evaluation practices. The work targets the right audience by explicitly addressing alignment problems of attention-only models and positioning itself against established ASR approaches, and the related work and writing quality are reported as thorough and good, indicating proper placement in the venue's research community. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses end-to-end ASR (joint CTC–attention), a topic squarely within top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by tackling core modeling issues (irregular attention alignments mitigated via CTC guidance) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) against appropriate baselines (hybrid HMM/DNN, CTC variants, coverage-term decoding). The methodology (multi-task CTC+attention training and joint decoding), reporting of hyperparameters, and thorough related-work coverage align with norms and expectations for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses end-to-end ASR via a joint CTC-attention model, which is squarely within the remit of top-tier NLP/ML conferences. The work targets the right audience by explicitly addressing alignment problems of attention-only models and positioning itself against established ASR approaches, and the related work and writing quality are reported as thorough and good, indicating proper placement in the venue's research community.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation seems consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which aids clarity and reproducibility. However, the summary does not specify evaluation metrics nor mention figures/tables or code availability, which are omissions that reduce clarity in experimental presentation. These are minor presentation issues, so I rate it as well-written with small areas for improvement. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with the method, experiments (CSJ and HKUST), and conclusions clearly presented; main contributions (joint CTC-attention training/decoding) are stated early and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain in the provided summary—notation consistency and the effectiveness of figures/tables are not reported, and evaluation metrics and code availability are unspecified—so while clarity is strong, I cannot judge it as exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation seems consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which aids clarity and reproducibility. However, the summary does not specify evaluation metrics nor mention figures/tables or code availability, which are omissions that reduce clarity in experimental presentation. These are minor presentation issues, so I rate it as well-written with small areas for improvement.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the method, experiments (CSJ and HKUST), and conclusions clearly presented; main contributions (joint CTC-attention training/decoding) are stated early and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain in the provided summary—notation consistency and the effectiveness of figures/tables are not reported, and evaluation metrics and code availability are unspecified—so while clarity is strong, I cannot judge it as exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR framework that explicitly combines CTC and attention objectives during training and decoding, which is a clear, novel integration of two distinct paradigms to address attention alignment failures (repetition/deletion). It differentiates itself from prior CTC-only and attention-only work by using CTC as alignment guidance and demonstrates this on CSJ and HKUST benchmarks against strong baselines. The technique is a non-trivial, practically motivated extension rather than a trivial combination, so it would be noteworthy to experts though not a radical new problem formulation. | B(4): The paper proposes a joint CTC–attention multi-task training and joint-decoding framework that explicitly combines known CTC and attention-based end-to-end ASR strengths, addressing attention models' irregular alignments (repetition/deletion) by using CTC as alignment guidance. The approach is clearly differentiated from prior single-paradigm CTC or attention-only methods and evaluated against strong baselines (CTC variants, hybrid DNN/HMM, TDNN, coverage-term decoding) on CSJ and HKUST. While the idea is a non-trivial and practically useful combination that would inform experts, it mainly integrates two existing techniques rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm, so the novelty is notable but not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR framework that explicitly combines CTC and attention objectives during training and decoding, which is a clear, novel integration of two distinct paradigms to address attention alignment failures (repetition/deletion). It differentiates itself from prior CTC-only and attention-only work by using CTC as alignment guidance and demonstrates this on CSJ and HKUST benchmarks against strong baselines. The technique is a non-trivial, practically motivated extension rather than a trivial combination, so it would be noteworthy to experts though not a radical new problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a joint CTC–attention multi-task training and joint-decoding framework that explicitly combines known CTC and attention-based end-to-end ASR strengths, addressing attention models' irregular alignments (repetition/deletion) by using CTC as alignment guidance. The approach is clearly differentiated from prior single-paradigm CTC or attention-only methods and evaluated against strong baselines (CTC variants, hybrid DNN/HMM, TDNN, coverage-term decoding) on CSJ and HKUST. While the idea is a non-trivial and practically useful combination that would inform experts, it mainly integrates two existing techniques rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm, so the novelty is notable but not revolutionary.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—irregular alignments (repetition and deletion) in attention-only end-to-end ASR—by proposing a joint CTC-attention training and decoding scheme that leverages CTC's monotonic alignment as guidance. It achieves comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems on CSJ and HKUST without external linguistic resources, indicating strong applicability and practitioner relevance, though it did not surpass TDNN with LF-MMI. By effectively combining CTC and attention, the work is likely to change how researchers design end-to-end ASR models and to open meaningful follow-up research directions, giving it a strong (but not transformative) impact. | B(4): The paper targets an important problem—irregular alignments (repetition/deletion) in attention-only end-to-end ASR and decoding limitations of CTC—by proposing joint CTC-attention training and decoding to combine monotonic alignment with flexible modeling. It achieves comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems on CSJ and HKUST without external linguistic resources, which could change how researchers and practitioners design end-to-end ASR systems. Because it did not surpass the TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training, the work is a strong, broadly useful contribution that opens significant follow-on work but is not fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—irregular alignments (repetition and deletion) in attention-only end-to-end ASR—by proposing a joint CTC-attention training and decoding scheme that leverages CTC's monotonic alignment as guidance. It achieves comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems on CSJ and HKUST without external linguistic resources, indicating strong applicability and practitioner relevance, though it did not surpass TDNN with LF-MMI. By effectively combining CTC and attention, the work is likely to change how researchers design end-to-end ASR models and to open meaningful follow-up research directions, giving it a strong (but not transformative) impact.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets an important problem—irregular alignments (repetition/deletion) in attention-only end-to-end ASR and decoding limitations of CTC—by proposing joint CTC-attention training and decoding to combine monotonic alignment with flexible modeling. It achieves comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems on CSJ and HKUST without external linguistic resources, which could change how researchers and practitioners design end-to-end ASR systems. Because it did not surpass the TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training, the work is a strong, broadly useful contribution that opens significant follow-on work but is not fully transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: evaluations on two realistic corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons against multiple strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC variants, TDNN with LF training, coverage-term decoding) support the main claims. However, the summary does not report ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., isolating the contribution of the CTC loss, varying interpolation weights, or decoding choices). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations are explicitly discussed (failure to surpass TDNN LF performance; attention alignment issues; CTC decoding constraints). Reproducibility is partly addressed by reporting hyperparameters, but no code is mentioned and evaluation metrics/details are not specified in the summary. | B(3): The paper runs experiments on two standard datasets (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several strong baselines (hybrid DNN-HMM, CTC variants, coverage-term decoding), which supports the main claims. However, evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned despite reporting hyperparameters, limiting assessment of which components drive gains. Related work coverage and discussion of limitations (e.g., not matching TDNN+LF-MMI, attention irregularities, CTC decoding needs) are thorough. Reproducibility is only partially supported (hyperparameters reported but code and explicit metrics/decoding details appear missing).

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: evaluations on two realistic corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons against multiple strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC variants, TDNN with LF training, coverage-term decoding) support the main claims. However, the summary does not report ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., isolating the contribution of the CTC loss, varying interpolation weights, or decoding choices). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations are explicitly discussed (failure to surpass TDNN LF performance; attention alignment issues; CTC decoding constraints). Reproducibility is partly addressed by reporting hyperparameters, but no code is mentioned and evaluation metrics/details are not specified in the summary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments on two standard datasets (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several strong baselines (hybrid DNN-HMM, CTC variants, coverage-term decoding), which supports the main claims. However, evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned despite reporting hyperparameters, limiting assessment of which components drive gains. Related work coverage and discussion of limitations (e.g., not matching TDNN+LF-MMI, attention irregularities, CTC decoding needs) are thorough. Reproducibility is only partially supported (hyperparameters reported but code and explicit metrics/decoding details appear missing).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong and relevant set of baselines (DNN-hybrid Kaldi recipe, CTC variants including CTC+LM, TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training, and coverage-term decoding) and evaluates on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST). It explicitly acknowledges when prior work performs better (stating it did not reach the TDNN LF-MMI result) and presents a clear explanation of how the joint CTC–attention design differs and mitigates attention-only failure modes. However, the summary does not confirm that all comparisons used identical evaluation metrics and conditions, so fairness of some comparisons is unclear despite reported hyperparameters and thorough related-work coverage. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate and recent baselines (hybrid DNN/HMM, CTC variants with and without LM, TDNN with lattice-free sequence training, and coverage-term decoding). It evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), but the provided summary does not specify evaluation metrics or fully confirm identical experimental conditions, leaving some questions about strict fairness. The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g., TDNN with LF-MMI) and clearly describe how the joint CTC-attention approach differs from those baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough and most relevant competing approaches are cited.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong and relevant set of baselines (DNN-hybrid Kaldi recipe, CTC variants including CTC+LM, TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training, and coverage-term decoding) and evaluates on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST). It explicitly acknowledges when prior work performs better (stating it did not reach the TDNN LF-MMI result) and presents a clear explanation of how the joint CTC–attention design differs and mitigates attention-only failure modes. However, the summary does not confirm that all comparisons used identical evaluation metrics and conditions, so fairness of some comparisons is unclear despite reported hyperparameters and thorough related-work coverage.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate and recent baselines (hybrid DNN/HMM, CTC variants with and without LM, TDNN with lattice-free sequence training, and coverage-term decoding). It evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), but the provided summary does not specify evaluation metrics or fully confirm identical experimental conditions, leaving some questions about strict fairness. The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g., TDNN with LF-MMI) and clearly describe how the joint CTC-attention approach differs from those baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough and most relevant competing approaches are cited.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claim — that attaching a CTC objective to an attention encoder–decoder and using joint CTC–attention decoding alleviates attention alignment problems and yields competitive results — is supported by experiments on two standard corpora (CSJ, HKUST) and comparisons to strong baselines (hybrid DNN, CTC variants, TDNN/LF-MMI). The methodology (multi-task CTC+attention training and combined decoding) is technically sound and appropriate for the stated goal. Minor issues are that evaluation details (exact metrics, statistical significance) are not specified in the summary and mathematical/derivational details are not reported here, so some experimental rigor/details are missing but do not undermine the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper’s main claim—that joint CTC-attention training and decoding improves robustness of attention models—is supported by experiments on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to several strong baselines, with hyperparameters reported. The proposed multi-task formulation and joint decoding are technically sound and require no novel or questionable derivations. Minor issues remain: the summary does not report specific evaluation metrics or statistical significance tests, nor an explicit ablation or alignment-specific analysis to directly verify the claimed reduction in repetitions/deletions; also the method did not outperform the best TDNN+LF sequence-discriminative baseline. These are shortcomings but do not undermine the core technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claim — that attaching a CTC objective to an attention encoder–decoder and using joint CTC–attention decoding alleviates attention alignment problems and yields competitive results — is supported by experiments on two standard corpora (CSJ, HKUST) and comparisons to strong baselines (hybrid DNN, CTC variants, TDNN/LF-MMI). The methodology (multi-task CTC+attention training and combined decoding) is technically sound and appropriate for the stated goal. Minor issues are that evaluation details (exact metrics, statistical significance) are not specified in the summary and mathematical/derivational details are not reported here, so some experimental rigor/details are missing but do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claim—that joint CTC-attention training and decoding improves robustness of attention models—is supported by experiments on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to several strong baselines, with hyperparameters reported. The proposed multi-task formulation and joint decoding are technically sound and require no novel or questionable derivations. Minor issues remain: the summary does not report specific evaluation metrics or statistical significance tests, nor an explicit ablation or alignment-specific analysis to directly verify the claimed reduction in repetitions/deletions; also the method did not outperform the best TDNN+LF sequence-discriminative baseline. These are shortcomings but do not undermine the core technical claims.

main_claims: Proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR that leverages benefits of both CTC and attention during training and decoding.
The joint CTC-attention approach alleviates irregular alignment issues of attention-only models (e.g., repetition and deletion) by using CTC-based alignment as guidance.
Applied to Japanese (CSJ) and Mandarin (HKUST) benchmarks, the proposed method achieves comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems without using external linguistic resources.
_summary.methodology: Attach a CTC objective to an attention-based encoder-decoder ASR model (multi-task training) and use joint CTC-attention probabilities during decoding to combine strictly monotonic CTC alignment benefits with flexible attention-based modeling.
datasets: HKUST Mandarin Chinese conversational telephone speech recognition (MTS)
baselines: CTC with language model (Miao et al., 2016)
TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al., 2016)
limitations: Did not reach the performance obtained by TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al., 2016).
Attention-based ASR can produce irregular alignments (repetition and deletion) which the joint method aims to mitigate.
CTC requires language models and graph-based decoding except in the case of very large training data (i.e., CTC alone has decoding limitations).
